name: python-ruby
description: Second reference cartridge: Python -> Ruby. Dogfoods the cartridge contract on a fresh language pair to show the engine is language-agnostic. Covers cognate / false-friend / alien-realization / novel / vanished from a Python learner's standpoint.

residue.note: Same keyword (`def`) and intent; Ruby closes the body with `end` where Python uses indentation.

narrative.celebrate: You already define functions with `def` — that word is the same in Ruby.

residue.note: Same intent (interpolate a value into a string); Ruby uses `#{}` and needs no `f` prefix.

narrative.celebrate: f-strings are second nature to you — interpolation carries straight over.
narrative.better: Drop the `f` and write `#{}` instead of `{}`.

from.surface: if items:  # empty list is falsy

to.surface: if items   # empty array is TRUTHY

residue.note: In Ruby only `nil` and `false` are falsy; `[]`, `""`, and `0` are all truthy. The Python habit of testing a collection for emptiness via truthiness silently breaks.

narrative.celebrate: You've leaned on truthiness to check for empty/zero for years — that's a real, useful instinct.
narrative.safety: In Ruby that instinct misfires: `if items` is true even when empty. Test emptiness explicitly.
narrative.example: Python: if items:  ->  Ruby: if items.any?   (or: unless items.empty?)

residue.note: Same intention (transform each element) realised through different machinery: a comprehension expression vs a method taking a block.

narrative.celebrate: You think in comprehensions — that mapping intent is exactly right.
narrative.better: Ruby spells it as a block passed to `map`; once it clicks, blocks are everywhere.
narrative.example: [f(x) for x in xs]   ->   xs.map { |x| f(x) }

residue.note: Python has no symbol type. The nearest habits — interned strings, enum members, dict keys — are not the same thing; learn the symbol fresh.

narrative.celebrate: Coming from Python you have great instincts for dict keys and enums.
narrative.safety: A Ruby symbol (`:status`) is its own immutable, interned identifier — not a string. Learn it as a new thing rather than mapping it onto strings.

residue.note: Ruby has no explicit `self` parameter; the receiver is implicit. The Python habit of writing `self` first in every method has nowhere to go — re-route it.

narrative.celebrate: Writing `self` first is muscle memory from Python — totally reasonable.
narrative.safety: In Ruby that parameter vanishes: methods take only their real arguments, and `self` is implicit (use it bare when you need the receiver).
narrative.example: Python: def m(self): ...   ->   Ruby: def m ... end
